title: How to Spot Fake Bank Text Messages and Protect Your Money
excerpt: Learn the red flags of fake bank text messages, what never to click, how to verify alerts safely, and what to do right away if you already responded.
relatedKeywords: how to spot fake bank text alerts
content:
If you have ever gotten a text that says your bank spotted fraud and you need to act now, pause before you tap anything. Fake bank text messages are one of the fastest ways scammers steal account access, card details, and personal information. They look urgent on purpose, and that urgency is exactly what makes them dangerous.

This kind of scam is often called smishing, short for SMS phishing. The message pretends to come from your bank, credit card company, or another financial institution. It may warn about a suspicious charge, a locked account, or a failed payment. The goal is simple: get you to click, reply, call, or share information before you slow down and verify what is happening.

The good news is that fake bank texts usually follow a small set of patterns. Once you know those patterns, they become much easier to spot. In this guide, you will learn what fake bank text messages look like, how to tell whether a bank alert is real, what to do if you already clicked, and how to protect your accounts going forward.

## Why fake bank text messages work so well

Text messages feel personal and immediate. Most people read them within minutes, and scammers know that. The FTC reported $330 million in losses to text scams in 2022, with median reported losses of $1,000. The same FTC analysis found that reports about texts impersonating banks rose sharply, and copycat bank fraud alerts were one of the top text scam types.

A fake bank text often works because it creates pressure. It might say:

- Your debit card has been locked
- A large purchase needs to be verified
- Unusual activity was detected
- Your account will be suspended unless you act now
- You need to confirm a transfer or payment

When people think their money is at risk, they move fast. Scammers want that. They want you to react before you remember a basic rule: a legitimate bank problem can always be checked through your bank's real app, website, or customer service number.

## What is a fake bank text message?

A fake bank text message is a phishing message that pretends to come from a financial institution. It is designed to trick you into doing one of four things:

1. Clicking a malicious link
2. Replying to confirm that your number is active
3. Calling a fake fraud number
4. Sharing passwords, card details, account numbers, or one-time codes

Some fake messages are sloppy and full of spelling mistakes. Others are very convincing. They may use your bank's name, copy the tone of a real fraud alert, or even appear in the same text thread as legitimate bank messages because of sender spoofing. That is why you should never decide a message is real based only on how familiar it looks.

## The most common red flags

### 1. The message creates extreme urgency

Phishing messages often try to rush you. The FTC warns that scammers commonly claim there is suspicious activity, a billing problem, or some urgent issue that requires immediate action. If a text pushes you to act right now, treat that pressure as a warning sign, not proof.

### 2. It asks you to click a link

This is one of the biggest red flags. Real companies may send links in some communications, but security guidance from the FTC and major banks is consistent on this point: do not click links in unexpected bank texts. If you need to check your account, open your bank app yourself or type the bank's website into your browser.

### 3. It asks for private information

Banks do not text you asking for your password, PIN, Social Security number, or one-time verification code. Wells Fargo says it will never ask for a password, PIN, or one-time access codes through email or text. City National Bank similarly warns that it will never ask for your online banking password or multifactor verification code by text.

### 4. The sender details do not match what your bank uses

Some banks send messages from specific short codes. Others do not text from ordinary ten digit numbers. If you receive a message from a random full phone number, an email address, or an unfamiliar sender format, be skeptical. Even then, spoofing is possible, so a matching sender is not enough to prove a message is real.

### 5. It asks you to reply YES or NO

The FTC says fake bank alerts often ask you to reply yes or no to verify a transaction. That reply tells the scammer a real person is engaged. From there, they may call pretending to be your bank's fraud department and try to get more information.

### 6. It tells you to call a number in the text

This is another classic move. The number goes to the scammer, not your bank. If you think the message might be real, call the number on the back of your debit card or credit card, or use the number listed inside your bank's official app or website.

### 7. The message has odd formatting or strange wording

Typos, awkward grammar, missing punctuation, and generic greetings like "Dear Customer" can all be clues. On their own, they do not prove fraud. Combined with urgency and a link, though, they are a strong sign that the message is fake.

## Can a real bank text you?

Yes. Many banks send legitimate texts for fraud alerts, transaction notifications, login verification, low balance alerts, and security codes. That is part of what makes these scams tricky. The problem is not that banks never text. The problem is that scammers imitate the texts banks send.

So the right question is not "Do banks text customers?" The better question is "How do I verify this specific message safely?"

## How to verify a bank text safely

If you receive a message about suspicious account activity, do this instead of tapping the text:

1. Close the message.
2. Open your bank app directly, or type your bank's web address yourself.
3. Check for alerts, transactions, or secure messages inside your account.
4. If you are still unsure, call the number on the back of your card or on the bank's official website.

Do not use the phone number, link, or QR code provided in the suspicious text. Wells Fargo specifically warns customers not to click links, scan QR codes, or call numbers from unexpected messages. That is solid advice for any bank, not just one institution.

## What scammers want from fake bank texts

A fake bank text is usually trying to lead to one of these outcomes:

### Stealing your login

The link may open a website that looks exactly like your bank's sign in page. If you enter your username and password, the scammer can try to log in for real.

### Stealing one-time codes

After getting your password, the scammer may ask for the verification code your bank texts to you. If you share it, you may hand them the final piece they need to enter your account.

### Installing malware

Some links or attachments can install malicious software on your phone or device. The FTC warns that suspicious links and attachments can deliver malware.

### Triggering a follow up phone scam

If you reply to the text, the scammer may call you while pretending to help. This follow up can sound very professional. They may ask for card numbers, passwords, or remote access to your device.

## What to do if you get a fake bank text

If you have not clicked or replied yet, keep it simple:

- Do not click the link
- Do not reply
- Do not call the number in the message
- Take a screenshot if you want a record
- Report the text
- Delete it

The FTC says you can forward scam text messages to 7726, which spells SPAM. You can also report the attempt at ReportFraud.ftc.gov. If the scam impersonates your bank, forward it to your bank's phishing reporting address if they provide one.

## What to do if you already clicked or replied

If you interacted with the message, move quickly. I am glad to catch this part early, because speed really matters here.

### If you clicked the link but did not enter anything

- Close the page immediately
- Do not download anything
- Run a security scan on your device if possible
- Update your phone and security software
- Monitor your bank account and cards closely

### If you entered your login or account information

- Change your bank password immediately using the real app or website
- Change any other accounts that reuse the same password
- Turn on multi-factor authentication if it is not already enabled
- Call your bank using an official number
- Ask the bank to review recent activity and add extra monitoring if needed

### If you shared a one-time code

Treat this as urgent. Contact your bank right away using the official fraud number. One-time codes are often the last step scammers need to access an account.

### If you gave away personal information

If the scammer got sensitive personal data, visit [IdentityTheft.gov](https://www.identitytheft.gov/) for a recovery plan. Depending on what was exposed, you may also want to freeze your credit. If you need help with that, read our guide on [how to freeze your credit](/blog/how-to-freeze-your-credit).

## How to protect yourself from future bank text scams

### Use your bank app as your source of truth

If a text claims there is a problem, confirm it in the app. This one habit will stop a huge number of phishing attempts.

### Turn on account alerts

Real transaction alerts can help you catch fraud quickly. They also make it easier to compare a suspicious text against the alerts your bank actually sends.

### Use strong unique passwords

A unique banking password limits the damage if another account is breached. A password manager can make this much easier.

### Enable multi-factor authentication

The FTC recommends multi-factor authentication because it makes account takeover harder. It is not perfect, especially if you give away the code, but it is still an important defense.

### Never share verification codes

Treat one-time codes like cash. If a person asks for one over text or phone, assume it is a scam.

### Keep your device updated

Security updates for your phone and apps help protect against malware and other attacks. Turn on automatic updates where possible.

### Review accounts regularly

The sooner you spot unauthorized activity, the easier it is to limit the damage. Pennywise can help here by making transaction review part of your normal money routine instead of something you only do when you feel worried.

## Real bank alert or fake? Use this quick checklist

When a bank text comes in, ask:

- Was I expecting this message?
- Is it pushing me to act immediately?
- Does it ask me to click, call, reply, or share a code?
- Can I verify the same issue in my real banking app?
- Am I using contact information I found myself, not from the text?

If the message fails that test, treat it as suspicious.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do banks ever text you about fraud?

Yes, some banks send fraud alerts by text. That is why you should verify alerts in your bank's official app or through a trusted phone number instead of relying on the text itself.

### Is replying STOP or NO safe?

Not always. In scam messages, any reply can confirm your number is active and may trigger follow up attempts. If the text looks suspicious, do not reply.

### What number should I call if I get a suspicious bank text?

Call the number on the back of your card, inside your official banking app, or on your bank's real website. Do not call the number in the text.

### Can fake bank texts appear in the same thread as real texts?

Yes. Sender spoofing can make scam messages look more convincing. That is why thread appearance alone is not enough to trust a message.

### What if I gave a scammer my banking password?

Change it immediately using the real bank website or app, then call your bank's fraud department using an official number and review your recent activity.

## Sources

- [FTC: How To Recognize and Avoid Phishing Scams](https://consumer.ftc.gov/articles/how-recognize-avoid-phishing-scams)
- [FTC Data Spotlight: The Top Text Scams of 2022](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/data-visualizations/data-spotlight/2023/06/iykyk-top-text-scams-2022)
- [Wells Fargo: Report Phishing and Identify Suspicious Messages](https://www.wellsfargo.com/privacy-security/fraud/report/phish/)
- [City National Bank: How to Identify and Avoid Bank Text Scams](https://www.cnb.com/personal-banking/insights/bank-text-scams.html)

## Final thoughts

A real bank wants you to protect your account. A scammer wants you to panic. That is the difference to remember when a text tries to rush you.

If you slow down, avoid the link, and verify through your real banking app or an official phone number, you will dodge most fake bank text scams. That one pause can save your password, your account access, and a lot of cleanup later.
